statement.markdown:
Given a string representing a nested HTML structure, your task is to fix the broken tags and return the corrected string. A tag is considered broken if it is not properly closed, or if it has an invalid closing tag. For example, `

Hello World

` is a broken tag because the `

` tag is not closed. Another example, `

Hello World

` is broken because the closing tag for `

` should be `

`. However, if a tag is properly closed like `

Hello World

`, it is considered valid.
statement.inputFormat: A string representing the nested HTML structure
statement.outputFormat: The corrected string with all broken tags fixed
statement.notes: You should not remove any valid tags, only fix the broken ones.

constraints.description: The number of characters in the input string is at most 10,000.

examples.explanation:
The closing tag for the
should be before the closing tag for the

The closing tag for the

should be before the invalid closing tag for

The closing tag for the
should be after the closing tag for the

hints.text: Use a stack data structure to keep track of the opening tags.
When you encounter a closing tag, check if it matches the top of the stack.

editorial.title: Fixing Nested HTML Tags
editorial.intuition: Use a stack data structure to keep track of the opening tags. When you encounter a closing tag, check if it matches the top of the stack. If it does, pop the top of the stack. If it doesn't, add the closing tag to the stack.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach: Parse the string character by character and check for valid closing tags.
editorial.betterSolution: Use a stack data structure to keep track of the opening tags.
editorial.optimalSolution: Iterate over the string and use a stack to keep track of the opening tags. When you encounter a closing tag, check if it matches the top of the stack and pop it if it does.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correct approach is to use a stack data structure to keep track of the opening tags and iterate over the string. This way, we can ensure that all tags are properly closed.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run: Start with an empty stack. Iterate over the string and add opening tags to the stack. When you encounter a closing tag, check if it matches the top of the stack and pop it if it does.
commonMistakes: Not using a stack data structure
Not checking if the closing tag matches the top of the stack
